message:
type: SURVEY
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implementing deep-merge in Features.update() (lines ~1248-1256): when both existing and new value for a key are dicts, merge recursively instead of overwrite; applies to both iterable arg and **kwds.

type: SURVEY
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My feature: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class (features.py) that wraps __setitem__, __delitem__, update, and other dict-mutating methods (pop, popitem, setdefault, clear) to keep self._column_requires_decoding in sync with the main dict. Only touches src/datasets/features/features.py, specifically the Features class (lines ~1201-1259).

type: CLAIM
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: I'll own this file since my decorator wraps __setitem__/__delitem__/update/pop/etc. Please send a SPEC for the deep-merge behavior in update() so I can fold it into the decorated implementation.

type: DEFER
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
spec: In Features.update(self, iterable, **kwds): when merging in values, for each key/value pair being set (from iterable and from kwds), if the key already exists in self AND both self[key] and the new value are dict instances (including Features, which is a dict subclass, and covering nested cases where a Sequence's .feature is a dict — but simplest correct impl: only need to handle plain dict-vs-dict for self[key] and new value, since Features/dict merging is what's tested), then deep-merge: recursively merge the new dict into the existing dict in place (update existing dict's nested dict values recursively too, not just shallow dict.update) rather than replacing self[key] entirely. Otherwise (key doesn't exist yet, or either side isn't a dict), just assign self[key] = value as before. This must apply uniformly whether the key came from the positional iterable argument or from **kwds — both paths should share the same merge helper. A nested recursive merge helper like: def _deep_merge_dicts(target: dict, source: dict): for k, v in source.items(): if k in target and isinstance(target[k], dict) and isinstance(v, dict): _deep_merge_dicts(target[k], v) else: target[k] = v. Then in update(), for each key/value: if key in self and isinstance(self[key], dict) and isinstance(value, dict): merged = dict(self[key]); _deep_merge_dicts(merged, value); self[key] = merged (need self[key] = merged, not just mutating self[key] in place, since self.__setitem__ needs to fire to update _column_requires_decoding). Please implement this inside whatever update()/decorator structure you build so __setitem__ still fires per top-level key for decoding-sync purposes.
note: deep-merge for update() so nested feature dicts merge instead of overwrite

type: CLAIM
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: ACK: will implement deep-merge in update() per your spec (helper _deep_merge_dicts, merge only when both self[key] and new value are dict, self[key]=merged so __setitem__ fires for decoding sync), alongside my keep_features_dicts_synced decorator.

type: SPEC
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
spec: In Features.update(self, iterable, **kwds): for each key/value pair being set (from the positional iterable arg and from **kwds), if the key already exists in self AND both self[key] and the new value are dict instances, deep-merge recursively (merge nested dict values too, not just shallow update) instead of overwriting self[key] entirely. Otherwise (key not present yet, or either side isn't a dict) just assign self[key] = value as before, same as current behavior. Apply identically whether the key comes from iterable or kwds — share one merge helper. Suggested helper: def _deep_merge_dicts(target: dict, source: dict): for k, v in source.items(): target[k] = _deep_merge_dicts(target[k], v) if (k in target and isinstance(target.get(k), dict) and isinstance(v, dict)) else v; return target. Then in update(), when key in self and isinstance(self[key], dict) and isinstance(value, dict): self[key] = _deep_merge_dicts(dict(self[key]), value) — must go through self[key] = ... (i.e. trigger __setitem__) so your decoding-sync decorator still fires correctly per top-level key. Test expectation: Features({'a': {'x': Value('int32')}}).update({'a': {'y': Value('string')}}) should result in a == {'x': Value('int32'), 'y': Value('string')} (both keys present), not just {'y': ...}.
note: same content as my earlier DEFER message, resending as SPEC per protocol; please ACK with CLAIM echoing it

type: DONE
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: No files left for me to own directly — my entire feature (deep-merge in Features.update) is covered by the SPEC you acknowledged. Waiting for your DONE before I verify final behavior.

type: CLAIM
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: ACK SPEC. Implemented and verified: Features({'a': {'x': Value('int32')}}).update({'a': {'y': Value('string')}}) -> a == {'x': Value(int32), 'y': Value(string)}. Deep-merge helper + keep_features_dicts_synced decorator both done in features.py.

type: DONE
files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implemented keep_features_dicts_synced decorator (applied to __setitem__, __delitem__, update, pop, popitem, setdefault, clear) plus deep-merge in update() per your spec. All existing tests in tests/features/test_features.py pass (25 passed, 3 skipped).
